title: Watercolour buttercream
subtitle: Blended food-colour buttercream for a painted cake finish
excerpt: A vanilla American buttercream divided into small portions, each tinted a different diluted food-colour shade. Daubs of colour are applied to an already-crumb-coated cake and blended into a soft watercolour wash with a warm palette knife or icing smoother. The technique produces a painted finish rather than a block-colour one.
sourceNotes: Watercolour cake decoration is a modern cake-decorating style that emerged in professional and home baking in the early twenty-first century. It applies the aesthetic of watercolour painting to buttercream-coated cakes, using thinned or blended food colouring to produce soft, washed-out tonal effects rather than the opaque block colours of traditional cake decoration. The technique draws on basic buttercream application skills adapted from standard American and Swiss meringue methods.

recipe.yieldDescription: enough to decorate one 20 cm two-layer cake
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The buttercream can be made up to 3 days ahead and kept refrigerated, covered. Bring to room temperature and re-beat before use. Apply the colour finish on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven; a no-bake decorating preparation applied to an already-baked cake

glossaryTerms.definition: The watercolour effect works by placing small daubs of differently tinted buttercream directly onto the base coat and then blending them together with a warm tool. A palette knife or icing smoother warmed briefly in hot water and wiped dry will glide across the surface without dragging. Work in one direction. The colours should blend at the edges without fully mixing; distinct patches of colour create the tonal variation that gives the effect its name. Chilling the base coat until firm before applying the colours prevents them from sinking in.

content.text: This recipe assumes a crumb-coated, chilled cake as the starting point. The
depends on the base coat being cold and firm before colours are applied. Use gel food colouring, not liquid: liquid colouring changes the buttercream consistency.
Make the buttercream and crumb coat

Beat 250 g softened butter in a stand mixer for 3 to 4 minutes until pale and fluffy. Add 500 g sifted icing sugar in three additions, beating well between each. Add 1 tsp vanilla and 2 tbsp milk and beat for 2 more minutes. Apply a thin crumb coat of plain white buttercream to the assembled cake, smoothing it with an icing smoother to trap any loose crumbs. Refrigerate for 30 minutes until the crumb coat is firm.

Divide the remaining buttercream into small portions and tint each with a different gel food colour, using a tiny amount at a time for pale, watercolour-like shades. Place the chilled cake on a turntable. Using an icing spatula, apply small irregular patches of each colour around the sides and top of the cake. Warm the icing smoother briefly in hot water, wipe dry, and sweep across the colours in one smooth motion to blend the edges together. Repeat with a warm spatula to fill gaps. The colours should remain distinct but blurred at the edges.

items.problem: Colours are blending into a single muddy tone
items.solution: Too many colours are being blended together, or the tool is moving over too large an area. Blend only adjacent colours at their edges. Leave pockets of each individual colour untouched.

items.problem: Buttercream is dragging and tearing rather than blending smoothly
items.solution: The base coat is not cold enough, or the smoother is not warm enough. Re-chill the cake for 15 minutes and re-warm the smoother. The warm tool glides over the cold buttercream without sticking.
